source: Curated representative subset. Canonical machine-readable export: https://asvs.dev
note: These entries cover the categories the current checks touch (access control, secrets, configuration, and injection/encoding). IDs follow the v5.0.0-CHAPTER.SECTION.REQUIREMENT format. Verify against the canonical export before relying on the exact IDs in published output; expand to the full standard in a follow-up.

requirements.title: Server-side enforcement of access control
requirements.text: Verify that the application enforces access control rules on a trusted service layer and never relies solely on client-side checks. Every server entry point that accesses protected data must verify the requester's identity and authorization before completing the operation.

requirements.title: Authorization checks at every entry point
requirements.text: Verify that authorization decisions are made at every function or endpoint that performs sensitive actions or returns sensitive data, including server actions, route handlers, and API routes. Missing authorization on a single entry point bypasses all other controls.

requirements.title: No hardcoded secrets or credentials in source
requirements.text: Verify that the application does not contain hardcoded secrets, API keys, or database credentials in source code, configuration files, or comments. Secrets must be sourced from a secret store or environment variables that are scoped to the runtime that needs them.

requirements.title: Server-only secrets not exposed to the client
requirements.text: Verify that secrets intended for server use only are not embedded in client bundles. In Next.js, environment variables prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_ are inlined into the browser bundle; high-privilege keys (service-role, signing, admin) must never carry that prefix.

requirements.title: Content Security Policy
requirements.text: Verify that the application sets a Content-Security-Policy header that restricts the origins from which scripts, styles, and other resources may be loaded, reducing the impact of cross-site scripting.

requirements.title: HTTP Strict Transport Security
requirements.text: Verify that the application sets a Strict-Transport-Security header that instructs browsers to use TLS for all future connections, with an appropriate max-age and includeSubDomains.

requirements.title: Clickjacking and MIME-sniffing protections
requirements.text: Verify that responses set X-Frame-Options (or a frame-ancestors CSP directive) to prevent clickjacking, and X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff to prevent MIME-type confusion attacks.

requirements.title: Safe, parameterized interaction with interpreters
requirements.text: Verify that the application protects against injection by using parameterized queries, prepared statements, or safe object-relational mappers for database access, and never builds a query or other interpreter input by concatenating or interpolating caller-controllable data. Code must not be constructed and executed dynamically (e.g. eval) from runtime strings.

requirements.title: Context-aware output encoding and sanitization
requirements.text: Verify that untrusted data is contextually encoded or sanitized before it is rendered. In particular, values rendered as raw HTML (e.g. via dangerouslySetInnerHTML) must be sanitized with a vetted sanitizer, or rendered as text, to prevent cross-site scripting.
